input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a 1/10‑scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensors and control hardware. It consists of a lower chassis, an upper chassis, and dedicated autonomy elements (e.g., perception sensors, actuators, and on‑board control units). The vehicle operates in a laboratory‑type test environment where adaptive cruise control (ACC) functionality can be exercised, making it a suitable platform for digital twinning due to its resource‑constrained ECUs, modular hardware layering, and need for off‑board computation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin system (DTS) provides three core services: (1) Data Monitoring – a UI displays real‑time vehicle state, projected driving path, and nearby object tracks, and allows users to view, save, and generate reports of the collected data; (2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm (lead‑vehicle detection, desired‑speed computation) is migrated from the on‑board ECU to the DT, which processes the data in the cloud and returns the desired speed for actuation; (3) Remote Management – the UI enables operators to enable/disable ACC, set a target speed, and issue an emergency braking command, all over a low‑latency communication link. These services target two user groups: operators (monitoring and remote control) and developers (debugging and data analysis).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation follows the ISO 23247 digital‑twin framework. Middleware is provided by Robot Operating System 2 (ROS 2) for message passing between vehicle ECUs and the twin. The twin runs on NVIDIA JetPack 5.0, using GPU‑accelerated compute for the offloaded ACC logic. Development uses Python 3.8 and C++ for ROS 2 nodes, while MATLAB/Simulink models generate and validate the ACC control algorithm. Cloud/edge infrastructure supplies low‑latency communication (UDP/TCP) to meet safety‑critical timing, and a relational database stores logged vehicle data for the monitoring UI.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system mandates that all participating devices share a common notion of time for computation offloading, requiring clock synchronization accuracy of 100 ms (the limit imposed by hardware). This synchronization level is set to satisfy safety‑critical vehicular application standards that often require stricter than 100 ms alignment.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: A single instance of each entity (Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity) is instantiated, with each entity deployed on separate hardware components. This reflects a decentralized deployment of functional entities rather than multiple, hierarchical twin instances; no further multiplicity or coordination among multiple twins is described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The architecture follows the ISO 23247 reference model: the Physical Twin Entity hosts sensors, actuators and low‑level processing; the Digital Twin Entity provides the digital representation and control logic; the User Entity supplies visualization and control‑panel interfaces. Each Functional Entity is realized as an individual ROS 2 package containing one or more ROS 2 nodes, and the packages are distributed across distinct compute nodes, using ROS 2 middleware for inter‑entity communication and overall system orchestration.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The ISO 23247 standard mentions that integration with external systems can be achieved via protocols such as OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA). While the present implementation does not specify a concrete external‑system link, the possibility of employing OPC UA for data exchange and interoperability is noted.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin includes a Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking model that processes raw LiDAR point clouds using DBSCAN clustering and then tracks the resulting clusters with a Kalman filter. An Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) control‑logic model consumes the tracked object data to compute desired speed and spacing commands. Sensor data are gathered by a Data Collection component that subscribes to ROS2 topics publishing LiDAR and depth‑camera frames. Processed object tracks and the ego‑vehicle state are persisted by a Data Storage component implemented as a SQL database. The models are linked such that the output of the clustering and tracking pipeline feeds the ACC model, and both models are exported as ROS2 messages for downstream consumption.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is performed by a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller driving the vehicle motor and a steering servo that adjusts wheel angle. The Actuation software component receives high‑level speed and steering setpoints from the Digital Twin Entity, converts them into motor voltage commands and servo PWM signals, and transmits these commands over the vehicle CAN bus via ROS2 nodes. Remote control is offered through the User Interface Control Panel, allowing operator‑initiated speed and steering adjustments within the actuator’s rated torque and angular limits. Safety constraints include a mandatory 100 ms synchronization bound for command propagation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing stack consists of a Hokuyo UST‑10LX 2‑D LiDAR (max range 10 m, 40 Hz scan rate) and an Intel RealSense D435i depth camera (RGB‑D, up to 30 Hz). Both sensors publish point‑cloud data to ROS2 topics; the LiDAR provides planar range scans while the depth camera supplies dense 3‑D point clouds. Data are transmitted over Ethernet/USB to the onboard compute platform, sampled at the sensors’ native frequencies, and synchronized to within 100 ms to support real‑time clustering and tracking.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor data from the physical twin (LiDAR point‑cloud, motor status, steering‑servo signals, and overall vehicle state) is transmitted to the digital twin over a Wi‑Fi link using UDP at the transport layer. Upon arrival the Data Translation functional entity serialises the streams, aligns message types and performs unit conversions. Within the digital twin the data is distributed via a ROS 2 middleware network. The raw LiDAR point‑cloud is pre‑processed by the Point‑Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component, converting individual points into clustered object tracks. The processed vehicle state, object tracks and projected driving path are then fed to the 3D‑visualisation, reporting and data‑monitoring services.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands generated by the Digital Twin (desired speed for ACC, ACC enable/disable signals, and emergency‑brake requests) are packaged by the Data Translation component, transmitted over the Wi‑Fi UDP link to the Physical Twin, and received by the vehicle’s actuation front‑ends (motor, steering servo). The DT does not specify additional validation, authentication, or acknowledgment mechanisms; command execution is implied by the actuation front‑ends applying the received values.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The physical‑digital twin link uses a Wi‑Fi network with UDP transport. Internal communication among digital‑twin software components is handled by a ROS 2 network. The system is required to support low end‑to‑end latency communication, but specific bandwidth, latency thresholds, reliability, redundancy or security mechanisms are not detailed in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The ACC algorithm is offloaded from the on‑board ECU to external computational resources constituting the digital twin (referred to as the DT system). The documents mention that the offloading can be to “better‑suited equipment, e.g., in the cloud,” implying a cloud or remote edge deployment, but they do not specify exact hosting platforms, storage capacities, scalability or availability mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The DT development follows the ISO 23247 framework, beginning with an architectural design phase where key software components are identified and their relationships to sensors/actuators are defined (Fig. 2, Tab. I). The identified components are then mapped onto Functional Entities (FEs) and each FE is realized as an individual ROS 2 package containing one or more ROS 2 nodes. After implementation, the packages are deployed on separate hardware units that correspond to the three ISO entities (User Entity, Digital Twin Entity, Physical Twin Entity). The lifecycle therefore consists of design → FE mapping → ROS 2 package implementation → distributed deployment, without explicit governance or transition management steps described.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering workflow follows the ISO 23247 Part 2 reference architecture. First, key software components are identified (as listed in Table I) and their relationships to sensors and actuators of the physical twin are defined. These components are then mapped onto Functional Entities (FEs) defined by the ISO standard. Each FE is implemented as an independent ROS 2 package; the package contains one or more ROS 2 nodes that realize the FE’s functionality. The mapping yields three main entities: the User Entity (hosting the User Interface FE split into 3D Visualization and Control Panel sub‑components), the Digital Twin Entity (hosting data‑management and control mechanisms), and the Physical Twin Entity (hosting data pre‑processing, actuation, and generic automotive functions such as point‑cloud clustering and tracking). The entities are deployed on separate hardware platforms, reflecting ownership of functionality. A Data Storage component is included although it does not naturally align with an existing FE. The process emphasizes modularity, adherence to the entity‑based structure, and deployment across distributed hardware, but the documents do not describe version‑control, testing, or validation practices.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The User Entity UI provides decision‑support capabilities. It visualises real‑time vehicle state data (e.g., speed, sensor point clouds) and allows the operator to **view, save and generate reports** of historical data. Through the Control Panel, the user can remotely manage the Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) system, issue commands such as start/stop ACC, and trigger safety actions like an **emergency‑braking command**. The DT aggregates raw sensor streams, applies pre‑processing (e.g., point‑cloud clustering & tracking) and presents processed metrics with confidence indicators, enabling the operator to assess the reliability of recommendations. Generated insights are exported as structured logs for post‑analysis and can feed higher‑level optimisation algorithms in a PLM context.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The system is explicitly designed to comply with the ISO 23247 Digital Twin framework for manufacturing, adopting the Part 2 reference architecture with its Observable Manufacturing Element, Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. The implementation respects industry timing standards for safety‑critical vehicular applications that mandate clock synchronization tighter than 100 ms, and the design enforces a 100 ms synchronization accuracy due to hardware constraints. No additional certification or interoperability standards are mentioned.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately reflects the physical twin described as a '1/10-scale autonomous vehicle' (Section I, III) built from a Traxxas teleoperated vehicle. It correctly identifies the structural components ('lower level chassis', 'upper level chassis') and the presence of 'autonomy components' such as sensors (LiDAR, Depth Camera) and control units (NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano, Electronic Speed Controller). The operational domain is correctly inferred as a laboratory test environment for ACC. The mention of 'resource-constrained ECUs' is supported by the text discussing the need to offload computation from them (Section III.A). The text supports the modular hardware layering and the suitability for digital twinning due to the specific ACC case study.

The extraction provides a highly detailed and accurate summary of the three services defined in Section III.A: 'Data Monitoring', 'Computation Offloading', and 'Remote Management'. The details for Data Monitoring (UI, real-time state, projected path, tracks, report generation) are explicitly supported. The Computation Offloading description (ACC algorithm moved to DT/Cloud, lead vehicle detection, speed computation returned to vehicle) matches the source text perfectly. The Remote Management details (enable/disable ACC, set speed, emergency braking) are also directly supported. The identification of user groups (operators and developers) and their specific uses is found in the text ('operators... to monitor and remotely manage ACC', 'developers... to troubleshoot').

The extraction accurately lists the technologies: ISO 23247 framework, ROS 2, NVIDIA JetPack 5.0, Python 3.8, C++, MATLAB/Simulink, and low-latency communication. It correctly describes their roles (middleware, SDK, development tools, etc.). The mention of a relational database for data storage is supported by Table I and the text. The extraction is comprehensive and specific. Quotes: 'Implementation follows the ISO 23247 reference architecture', 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware to integrate application modules', 'We use NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK) for programming the Jetson Orin Nano', 'Application modules are implemented in Python 3.8... C++... MATLAB/Simulink', 'Data Storage Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database.'

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the specific synchronization requirement and its justification found in the DTS Requirements section. It correctly states the 100 ms accuracy is needed for computation offloading and due to hardware limitations, while noting stricter standards for safety-critical apps. Quotes: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms.', 'To enable computation offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.', 'We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.'

The description correctly identifies the deployment of entities on separate hardware and the singular nature of the instance in this case study. The text supports the separation of entities onto different hardware components. Quotes: 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component', 'User-facing components are organized into the User Entity', 'SW components that implement data management and control mechanisms are organized into the Digital Twin Entity'.

The extraction provides a highly detailed and accurate summary of the architecture based on ISO 23247 and its implementation using ROS 2. It correctly maps entities to their functions and describes the specific software packaging strategy. Quotes: 'implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes', 'Physical Twin Entity... reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators)', 'User Entity... provides interfaces for users to interact with the Digital Twin Entity'.

The extraction correctly notes that the ISO 23247 standard mentions OPC UA as a possibility for implementation (specifically for the Data Translation FE) and that the specific case study implementation relies on a custom ROS2 network without a specified external link. Quotes: 'one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture (UA) of IEC 62541 to facilitate the implementation of the Data Translation FE, or implement a custom communication layer', 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network'.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking model (DBSCAN, Kalman filter), the ACC model (computes desired speed), data sources (LiDAR, depth camera, vehicle state), and data storage (SQL database). It correctly describes the flow of data between models and their export as ROS2 messages. Quotes: 'Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking Clusters raw LiDAR point clouds via DBSCAN, then tracks using a Kalman filter', 'Data Storage Gathers object tracks and ego vehicle state into a SQL database', 'ACC Determines lead vehicle and computes following speed'. The extraction is specific and well-grounded, but could include more depth on model relationships within the DT constellation, hence a 4.

The extraction correctly names the actuators (Vedder Electronic Speed Controller, steering servo), describes their control (motor voltage commands, servo PWM signals), and mentions remote control via the Control Panel. It also includes safety constraints (100 ms synchronization). Quotes: 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller', 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands', 'Control Panel Interface for the user to send remote management commands', 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms'. The extraction is detailed but could expand on actuation ranges or limitations mentioned in the text (e.g., performance class I ACC), so a 4 is appropriate.

The extraction provides precise details on sensor types (Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, Intel RealSense D435i), their specs (max range 10 m, 40 Hz, up to 30 Hz), and data transmission (ROS2 topics, Ethernet/USB). Quotes: 'Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR', 'Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera', 'publish point-cloud data to ROS2 topics'. The extraction is highly specific and fully supported by the text, earning a 5.

The extraction claims 'Not in Document'. The characteristic requires descriptions of model fidelity requirements, validation and verification methods, uncertainty sources, and error detection mechanisms. The source text mentions general definitions ('real-time and high-fidelity') and functional compliance ('implement ACC, as outlined in ISO 15622'), but does not describe specific validation/verification methods, uncertainty quantification, or error correction for the Digital Twin models. Thus, the relevant specific information is absent.

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. _A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

block_5_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture


1 [https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)
2 [https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU](https://youtu.be/Rv-sJJQc3WU)

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

block_6_judge.reasoning: The source document does not contain any information regarding data governance, privacy protection, consent management, or data sharing agreements. The term 'ownership' appears only in the context of 'ownership of the functionality' related to the engineering process of the Digital Twin System, which is distinct from data ownership and privacy.

While the text mentions that the system is 'safety-critical' and defines requirements for network latency (R7) to ensure real-time performance, it does not provide details on specific security measures (e.g., cybersecurity protocols, access control, authentication) or specific safety strategies (e.g., fail-safe mechanisms, threat mitigation) for the Digital Twin itself. The mentions of safety are largely contextual rather than descriptive of implemented or designed security and safety considerations.
